issue_title: R-Studio Basic Install leaves source window not working
issue_body:
I installed the latest download of Anaconda today, then used it to install the standard version of Rstudio.

Rstudio opens, and I can update packages, run basic code etc. However, the source window does not show any code/text, no matter what kind of file I open. 

I tried to install R-Studio separately and run off of Anaconda's installation of R, but that ran into other issues that I believe the conda team already knows about.... (it couldn't auto-find the R install, and when I navigated to anaconda's R install, it's missing lots of library files)

I tried to find some hint of what might be going wrong in lots of other places...but couldn't seem to find anything. Since I imagine this is a basic requirement for R-Studio, obviously others aren't having this issue...

issue_title: conda update .. --no-deps wants to remove dependencies
issue_body:
Using conda 4.4rc, when trying to update a single package with `--no-deps`, it actually wants to remove a whole lot of (unrelated) packages:

```
joris@joris-XPS-13-9350:~/scipy$ conda update shapely --no-deps

  environment location: /home/joris/miniconda3

    package                    |            build

    shapely-1.6.2              |           py35_1         703 KB  conda-forge

Proceed ([y]/n)?

I don't see this behaviour when downgrading conda to 4.3

issue_title: BUG: conda 4.0.4 fails to correctly resolve build numbers
issue_body:
Somewhere between `conda-4.0.2` and `conda-4.0.4` a bug has slipped in the dependency resolving logic.

One of my packages depends on gnuplot indirectly via a second dependency. There are multiple builds available in the bioconda channel:

```
gnuplot-5.0.3-0
gnuplot-5.0.3-1
gnuplot-5.0.3-2
gnuplot-5.0.3-3
```

conda-4.0.2 correctly identifies `gnuplot-5.0.3-3` as the package to pull in. For `>= conda-4.0.4`, conda erroneously thinks `gnuplot-5.0.3-0` is the correct dependency. 

`conda install gnuplot` correctly identifies `gnuplot-5.0.3-3` as the correct version for both versions.

issue_title: stack trace with `conda clone` if package doesn't exist
issue_body:
I have a bundle with an `info/index.json` file that contains this entry in its `depends` list:
```
    "fiona 1.1.2 py27_0",
```

When I use `conda clone` to re-create this, I get a KeyError from `conda.plan`:

![screenshot 2014-04-24 11 24 12](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/293525/2791380/33983a14-cbc6-11e3-95f6-93f5ab51915a.png)

https://www.dropbox.com/s/2ztva1zekiv6jj0/Screenshot%202014-04-24%2011.24.12.png

I'm using conda 3.4.2 on Wakari.

This error appears to be because `fiona 1.1.2` does not exist in the default conda repositories, in particular it is not here:

http://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/

1.1.3 and 1.1.4 are there, which makes it look a little like 1.1.2 may have been there once upon a time (when the bundle was created) but has since been removed. (is that possible? if so, that isn't so good!)

In any case, the stack trace shouldn't be handed back to the user, and since it is quite reasonable that some arbitrary dependency list is not necessarily going to specify packages that some subsequent user can "see", this condition should be handled more gracefully.  Maybe conda options to mask/ignore part (or all) of a package spec versioning would be nice: `--ignore-patch`  `--ignore-minor` `--ignore-major`.

It would probably be nice to expose the underlying error messages when somebody gets a CommandError. Right now we just show the code and message, but it would be nice to show whatever is in the 'errors' array at `result[-1]['data']['errors']` (if it exists), as those errors are usually the most explanatory. Perhaps they can automatically by joined into a single string—your call.

It would also be nice to store the remainder of the 'data' object in the CommandError (minus the inserted `enable` or `config terminal` commands), so people can see the responses of the other commands run before the error hit.

issue_title: UVM support: alllow SV testbenches without test cases
issue_body:
UVM testbenches are widely used for verification, and Vunit could be used as a flow-controller for such methodologies, if some adaptations are made.

UVM testbenches have a top-level SystemVerilog module which has a slightly different style than a Vunit-based SystemVerilog testbench. Nevertheless, it would be nice to add the support for this style either using SystemVerilog or Python-based adaptations.
Here are the differences:

- The toplevel module which is passed to the simulator command calls a run_test (from the UVM base), which dynamically creates the class-based testbench hierarchy. The argument for this function is specified at run-time using the simulator argument +UVM_TESTNAME=<testcase>. Testcases/testsuites are not declared within the top-module in the SystemVerilog world.
- Constrained-randomization based testbenches require a seed value to be passed from the simulation command-line at run-time. This cannot be specified within the SystemVerilog/HDL code, and therefore need to come from the Python world
- The simulation stop command is issued by a $finish task deep in the UVM base-classes. So, a testcase run actually ends the simulation without returning control back to the Vunit runner. Therefore, the Vunit runner assumes that the test simulation just stopped unexpectedly
- UVM has its own messaging/logging/verbosity. A pass/fail test result is decided by parsing the UVM_ERROR and UVM_FATAL messages within the simulation transcript/log
- a test-suite description contains the testcase name, along with the number of random seeds it has to be simulated with. The list of seeds may also be specified with fixed values. The testcase along with the seed can be seen as a test-configuration in Vunit terms. This would ideally be in the Python world (for example in a UVM TB specific run.py file).

regardless I already wrote some memory benchmark that compares it to `streaming_bulk`, maybe we can develop a multithreaded bulk helper instead of the multiprocess one I think this would be useful. Or is there a way to use `parallel_bulk` without this excessive memory usage?

---

```python
import os
from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch
from elasticsearch.helpers import streaming_bulk, parallel_bulk
import psutil
es = Elasticsearch()
try:
    es.indices.create(index='my_test')
except:
    pass

def test(bulk_fn, size):
    def generator():
        for num in range(size):
            yield {
                    '_index': 'my_test',
                    '_id': num,
                    '_type': 'document',
                    '_source': {'some_id': num, 'data': 'A'*1024},
                    }

    i = 0
    for ok, result in bulk_fn(es, generator()):
        if i % (size//5) == 0:
            process = psutil.Process(os.getpid())
            mem = process.memory_info().rss / float(2 ** 20)
            print('item: %d, memory: %.3f MiB' % (i, mem))
        i += 1
    print('----')

print('parallel_bulk (50000):'); test(parallel_bulk, 50000)
print('parallel_bulk (100000):'); test(parallel_bulk, 100000)
print('parallel_bulk (200000):'); test(parallel_bulk, 200000)
print('streaming_bulk (50000):'); test(streaming_bulk, 50000)
print('streaming_bulk (100000):'); test(streaming_bulk, 100000)
print('streaming_bulk (200000):'); test(streaming_bulk, 200000)

es.indices.delete(index='my_test')
```

issue_title: 'az resource update' gets confused if property value contains an equal sign
issue_body:
e.g. consider the following command line, which works fine:

    az resource update --resource-group MyRG --namespace Microsoft.Web --parent sites/MySite --resource-type Extensions --name MSDeploy --set properties.packageUri=https://foo.com/bar.zip --api-version 2015-08-01

But now suppose the url I want to use for the packageUri has a query string, e.g.

    az resource update --resource-group MyRG --namespace Microsoft.Web --parent sites/MySite --resource-type Extensions --name MSDeploy --set properties.packageUri=https://foo.com/bar.zip?a=5 --api-version 2015-08-01

This fails with:

> Couldn't find 'packageUri=https://foo' in 'properties.packageUri=https://foo'. Available options: ['complete', 'deployer', 'endtime', 'provisioningstate', 'starttime']

The = sign is confusing the parsing logic. I tried quoting in various way, but did not find one that worked. e.g same error with `--set properties.packageUri="https://foo.com/bar.zip?a=5"`

PS: relates to [this SO question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/42894955/clear-an-app-service-instance-and-upload-new-content-from-a-zip-file/42895510#42895510)

issue_title: Improve experience adding/removing VMs/NICs from load balancers
issue_body:
Couple of suggestions for improving the experience when working with load balancers. Or set me straight if I'm just doing it all wrong :)

To add or remove a VM from the load balancer, you actually work with a virtual NIC resource. Logically, and architecturally, this makes sense. But really, it's the VM you're working on.

`az vm create` allows you to specify `--nsg` or `--subnet` to hook a VM up to existing network resources. Adding the option for `--lb-name` and `--lb-backend-pools` would be awesome. Or am I missing how to create a VM, let a new virtual NIC be created, and add it to a load balancer? Now it seems like you have to create the NIC with:

```
az network nic create \
  --resource-group myResourceGroup \
  --name myNic \
  --vnet-name myVnet \
  --subnet mySubnet \
  --network-security-group myNetworkSecurityGroup \
  --lb-name myLoadBalancer \
  --lb-address-pools myBackEndPool
```

And then create the VM with `az vm create --nics myNic [...]`

Kind of along the same lines, adding or removing a VM from a load balancer means working with the virtual NIC resource. Cool, a VM could have more than virtual NIC, but then it seems like I have to obtain the ID for a virtual NIC, strip to get just the name, and then remove or add from the load balancer. I can get the ID for a virtual NIC from a VM, but `az network nic ip-config address-pool remove` or `address-pool add` wants a NIC **name**, not **ID**. So I end up with:

```
vmnic=$(az vm show --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myVM \
    --query [networkProfile.networkInterfaces[*].id] --output tsv | sed 's:.*/::')

az network nic ip-config address-pool remove \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --nic-name $vmnic \
    --ip-config-name ipConfig1 \
    --lb-name myLoadBalancer \
    --address-pool myBackEndPool 
```

Or again, am I missing an easier way here? 

Suggestion would be to either

- allow `az network nic ip-config add | remove` to accept something like `--nic-id` rather than `--nic-name`, or 
- allow for `--vm-name` and then take the first virtual NIC attached to the VM.

issue_title: [DNS] Clarify help text when adding CNAME record
issue_body:
Consider:

C:\Users\jonatul>az network dns record cname add -h

Command
    az network dns record cname add

Arguments
    --cname             [Required]: Canonical name.
    --record-set-name   [Required]: The name of the record set relative to the zone. Creates a new
                                    record set if one does not exist.
    --resource-group -g [Required]: Name of resource group.
    --zone-name -z      [Required]: The name of the zone.


CNAMEs are a special case. You can only have a single CNAME record in a record set. Hence if there's already a CNAME record set and it contains a record, that existing record is REPLACED.

This needs to be made clear in the help text.

Suggested:

--record-set-name [Required]: The name of the record set relative to the zone. Creates a new record set if one does not exist. If a matching record already exists, it is replaced.

issue_title: On Python 2, upgrading from 0.1.0b10 to 0.1.0b11 may cause 'KeyError: Azure' error
issue_body:
On Python 2, it's recommended to upgrade with the `--ignore-installed` flag:
`pip install --upgrade --ignore-installed azure-cli`.

If the above isn't followed, KeyError may appear as pip fails to install 2 required files.
Add these manually with:
```
touch <path_to_site_packages>/azure/cli/__init__.py
touch <path_to_site_packages>/azure/cli/command_modules/__init__.py
```

<path_to_site_packages> is the path to the environment the CLI was installed into.
For example /usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages.

This is a one-time change.

Fresh installs are not affected.

---
More info:

Upgrading from the previous version of the CLI to this version breaks on Python 2.
It breaks because pip attempts to uninstall the old version first so https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/1406 occurs again.
Python 3 works because of implicit namespace package support.

### Description

When running a zone import e.g.:

```
az network dns zone import -g my-rg -n mydomain.com -f mydomain-zonefile.txt
```

where `mydomain-zonefile.txt` has the following content:

```
@     600     IN      SOA     ignored.com. hostmaster.mydomain.com. (
                                2017040400
                                28800
                                7200
                                604800
                                600
                                )

test    3600    IN      A       2.2.2.2
```

The relative domain record "test" is imported into Azure DNS as absolute value `test.mydomain.com` instead of relative value `test`, meaning that lookup `test.mydomain.com.mydomain.com` works and `test.mydomain.com` fails.

issue_title: Specifying the maximum joint velocities in the position control.
issue_body:
Currently there is no arguments in the position control for specifying the maximum joint velocity when controlling the robot arm, which is an important feature in many real-world robot APIs. I thought the pybullet might take the velocity limit specified in the URDF file of the robot. However, I found changing the velocity limit in the URDF does not make any differences, in terms of the time it takes to control the arm to  aset of specified target positions. I even tried to set the velocity limit to be all 0, and the arm still moves really fast.

I found setting maximum joint velocity would be an important feature. For example, the Intera SDK of the Sawyer robot lets user specify the maximum joint velocity in position control, and set it to be 0.3 of the maximally allowed joint velocity. Currently, without setting the maximum joint velocity, the Sawyer robot moves to any position in around 0.01~0.05 sec, while in the real world it takes 1~5 sec, which is very unrealistic. 

I was wondering what is the current way for Bullet to set the joint velocity during position control? Is there any way for us to specify the maximum velocity? If not, would it be possible for Erwin and Yunfei to add such a feature for more realistic robot control?  Thanks.

issue_title: Connector timeout includes time waiting for connector from pool
issue_body:
## Long story short

Typically connector timeouts means time waiting for a connection to a server, and does not include any time waiting for a connection from the pool

This is because the timeout is started here: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/client.py#L289

and in connect it will wait for a connector: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/connector.py#L382

## Expected behaviour

conn_timeout should only include active time waiting to connect to a server as this isn't an error and expected behavior.  I'd suggest moving the timeout to the connector. 

## Actual behaviour

If you make a large number of requests, and have limited number of connectors, your requests may timeout w/o even trying to connect to the server because no connectors are available.

## Steps to reproduce

create a large number of requests with a limited number of connectors with short conn_timeout.

## Your environment
Any platform with aiohttp 2.x

issue_title: TCP not closing properly after POST
issue_body:
## Long story short

I'm connecting to an IOT device (airconditioner) with a REST interface.

Posting commands using >=3.0 doesn't work. The post goes through and the status OK is returned, but there's some issue with the TCP connection. 

## Steps to reproduce

Within the [wireshark packet capture](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/files/2306190/AIO-Issue.zip) there's two different TCP transactions with exactly the same aiohttp code running (also within the zip file). The short version of the code without imports etc looks like this:

```python
device_ip = '10.0.0.107'

async def send_command_async(command: str, data: Any):
    body = {command : data}
    url = f"http://{device_ip}/{command}"
    async with ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.post(url, json=body) as response:
            response.raise_for_status()

loop: AbstractEventLoop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(loop.create_task(send_command_async('SystemMODE', 'heat')))
```

If you get aiohttp<3.0 you get the first packet capture, up until packet 10. Everything is happy.

With the latest version and any version above 3.0, all goes well until the ACK gets sent for the OK packet, then a whole spume of out of order and dup ack packets goes back and forth.

I can't actually see any difference in what gets sent or anything. It's possible it's the device that's the issue, I've emailed the guys that produce the device so they are on this issue too, but the low level TCP stuff is a bit beyond me.

The odd thing is that http GET works just fine.

## Environment

* Python 3.6
* Windows 10
* Latest aiohttp (but did a regression test back until <3.0 version)
* The device is an [iZone air conditioner](http://izone.com.au/)

At the moment the query runtime of `timeseries_for` is too high. After investigation, this query is slow since we fetch all data points for all segments. It could be improved by spliting it in multiple subqueries, one per partition.

Right now many APIs look like 
```
for image in (await docker.images.list()):
        print(f" {image['Id']} {image['RepoTags'][0] if image['RepoTags'] else ''}")
```
That's work and might be useful sometimes but I consider it ugly.
Better notation should be:
```
async for image in docker.images.list():
        print(f" {image['Id']} {image['RepoTags'][0] if image['RepoTags'] else ''}")
```
It's more native and doesn't require extra parenthesis.

Implementation should support both ways, sure.
It could be done by returning `Context` class from `docker.images.list()` non-coroutine call.
The context should support `__await__` and `__aiter__` for returning an async iterator with `__anext__`.

It's not very hard task but I have no time for it now unfortunately.
@achimnol would you do it? I'll be happy to review if needed.

[botocore.signers](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/signers.py) are not asynchronous. Not end of the world but we might as well make them async.

If you guys have any opinion on how you want to go about tackling this I'm more than up for giving it a go.

Ubuntu 16

`nbgrader` wants everything to be in the `$HOME` directories of the instructor and students.

Follow the wiki instructions to the letter and deploy with jupyterhub.

issue_title: matplotlib plot does not fully render if an exception is raised before cell execution completes
issue_body:
I run the following in a notebook cell:
```
%matplotlib notebook
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def plot_something():
    plt.figure()
    plt.plot([0,2],[0,2],'-')
    plt.show()

plot_something()
raise Exception
```

Expected output: 
- an interactive figure created with the line x==y on range [0,2]
- exception output (including a traceback).

Actual output:
- an interactive figure is created, but the line is not rendered/visible
- exception output (including traceback).

![notebook_matplotlib_exception](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/721123/22779232/9821297e-eec2-11e6-872b-806b7d83a04d.jpg)

Looking at the x,y prompt on the bottom right of the figure widget (attached screenshot) you can see that the axis range was set to [0,2] for both x & y. I have verified that by changing the plot parameters and checking that the top right corner x,y values change accordingly.

This issue is problematic when a cell executes a calculation which creates multiple figures. Any exception raised within the cell will prevent any plot from properly rendering, including the plots created before the exception was raised.

The issue is also exacerbated when the exception is raised in a following cell which gets invoked before the cell creating the plots completes execution. This is described in more detail in the [SO question I posted](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/40123739/force-matplotlib-to-fully-render-plots-in-an-ipython-jupyter-notebook-cell-befor).

I am not sure whether this belongs here or on matplotlib issues. I was unable to find a duplicate for this searching the issues for both.

Thanks!

issue_title: ESC or ^M to go to command-mode does not work on iOS
issue_body:
I'm using jupyter notebook on a AWS server or on a local machine. When I connect to the notebook with an iOS device (iPad Pro), most of the features work well. However, the keybindings do not work well, unfortunately. 

* `esc` or `^m` to switch from edit mode to command mode does not work. The keystroke `^m` simulates pressing `enter` (newline).
* `tab` does not code completion, but jumps the cursor to the next cell.

The problem seems browser, keyboard and Jupyter Notebook version independent. It was tested on an iPad Pro 12.9" (iOS 10.3) with Safari and Chrome, iPad Pro smart keyboard (without esc key) and regular keyboard, connected by bluetooth, on Jupyter Notebook version 4.2.1 and the latest version 5.0.0.

issue_body:
In version 10, there's no more `pool` object, so we need to change a lot of openupgradelib functions to adapt to the new architecture. The problem here is the backwards compatibility. I think right now in several possibilities:

* Create a separate branch for openupgradelib v10 version. This one is the cleanest option.
* Add keyword arguments for passing `env` and adapt code to detect not null values.
* Some kind of decorator to change the signature.

What do you think, @hbrunn, @StefanRijnhart, @jbeficent ?

When the BaseCradminInstance class was created, we only considered it for authenticated views. We use it for much more, and workarounds for other use-cases have been added.

We should create a class hierarchy that look something like this:

```python
class AbstractUiInstance:
    """
    The common API and common functionality.
    """


class LoginRequiredPreMixin:
    """
    Mixin class for :class:`.AbstractCrInstance` that requires the requesting
    user to be an authenticated user.
    """


class LoginNotRequiredPreMixin:
    """
    Mixin class for :class:`.AbstractCrInstance` that does not require
    the requesting user to be an authenticated user.
    """


class PublicUiInstance(LoginNotRequiredPreMixin, AbstractUiInstance):
    """
    No login, no roles.
    """


class AuthenticatedUiInstance(LoginRequiredPreMixin, AbstractUiInstance):
    """
    Login required, no roles.
    """


class RoleRequiredPreMixin:
    """
    Mixin class for :class:`.AbstractCrInstance` that provides
    a role (through ``request.cradmin_role``) to all views.
    """


class AuthenticatedRoleUiInstance(RoleRequiredPreMixin,
                                  LoginRequiredPreMixin,
                                  AbstractUiInstance):
    """
    Login required, role required.

    Replaces the old ``BaseCradminInstance``.
    """
```

Notice that the base class is called ``UiInstance`` (with abstract prefix). This indicates that the base class has nothing to do with roles or admin.
Just like #28, but only for time.

issue_title: to_n_ref() not filling with 0 for missing
issue_body:
`GenotypeArray.to_n_ref()` is sometimes outputting 1 for positions which are uncalled when fill is not given or set to 0.  Specifying a different fill returns that value as expected.
The docs for the function say that it should return 0 (default fill=0), but that is the one case which doesn't work.
ETA: `to_n_alt()` returns 0 for that position.  However, it has the same odd behavior as `to_n_ref`, just at different loci.

```
type(gfr)
allel.model.chunked.GenotypeChunkedArray

gfr.is_missing()[17,37]
True

gfr.is_called()[17,37]
False

gfr.to_n_ref()[17,37]
1

gfr.to_n_ref(fill=0)[17,37]
1

gfr.to_n_ref(fill=-1)[17,37] # works for any value I try other than 0
-1

gfr.to_n_alt()[17,37]
0
```

ETA: I'd like to be more helpful, but I can't actually find where that function is implemented in the code.

There is currently no way to see what files are included in a project from the command line.
For deciding to delete or using the --include/--exclude options with download/handover it would be beneficial to see this information.

You can see the list of projects with:

```
ddsclient list
```

I am considering adding an optional `-p <project_name>` to `list`that will print out the list of paths. So to print out the files in project `mouse`:

```
ddsclient list -p mouse
```

Another option would be to create a new command: `ddsclient list_contents -p <project_name>`.

Example possible output (format similar to `tree -if`/`find`):

```
peak
peak/e14.5-brain.ctcf.peak.txt
peak/e14.5-brain.enhancer.txt
peak/e14.5-brain.h3k27ac.peak.txt
peak/e14.5-brain.h3k4me3.peak.txt
peak/e14.5-brain.polII.peak.txt
peak/newfile.txt
results
results/data.dat
results/ng.2443-F3.jpg
results/result.docx
scripts
scripts/create_results.sh
```

There is a function [`np.take`](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.10.0/reference/generated/numpy.take.html), for which I think the definition of the gradient is zero, because it behaves like `np.oneslike`, `np.iscomplex` etc., in that there's no mathematical operation, but simply a rearrangement of data. Would this be correct?

I'm asking because I am using the function, and want to see if I can add this function to `autograd.numpy`, and put in a PR...?

issue_title: Add ability to specify device init script
issue_body: Please provide a way to specify a script that runs after device has been initialized. Script should be able to do things like install packages via apt, start services etc.

issue_title: Add the ability to git clone a module.
issue_body:
I love that I can build a yaml file like this

    imports:
        test_module: test_module/

     git module test_module:
        url: git@github.com:user/test_module.git

 and run `peru sync` to grab this (and the many more submodules) I'd like to use for this project. More and more I find that I want to break projects into separate repositories with their own revision history.

I would love to have the option for peru to git clone the test_module into the target directory. This means I can add the target directory to my `.gitignore`, work on the super project while using the sub project (test_module in this case) and I have the option to work on the sub project if the super project needs a feature added / changed.

Basically this boils down to I want to decouple my sub projects from my super projects. Just because my super project needs to depend on all of it's sub projects does not mean those sub projects need to be pulled into the super projects source tree (git subtree) or tied to a single commit of each sub project (git submodules)

In my mind I love what sync does but I'd like to change how it initializes things.

issue_title: Resolver should try all available data locations
issue_body:
(This issue should be addressed after the resolver is merged with the server)

The resolver currently gives up after trying the first location listed in the zone_file. 

CLI v0.0.13 however tries all listed locations. The resolver should be updated to follow that process as well.

issue_title: Plan to drop support for Python 2.7
issue_body:
Python 2.7 (and therefore all Python 2) reaches EOL on January 1st, 2020.

* https://github.com/python/devguide/pull/344

Pylast will drop support for Python 2.7 before that, perhaps this year. There will still be an installable Python 2.7 version on PyPI, but no further updates will be made for 2.7.

issue_title: Bash "reactive" files, do not raise error when charms.reactive.sh not present
issue_body: the basic layer doesn't appear to ship charms.reactive.sh, nor does it attempt to pip install it. The example 'which charms.reactive.sh` will fail on every deploy. I propose that we embed the charms.reactive.sh in the base layer, and copy it to /usr/local/bin as the pip install would, but as part of the base layer installation.

We need a way to share code between interface layers.

# `rest-api` extends `http`

Consider a database WololoDB® with an HTTP REST API. The charm for WololoDB should implement the generic `http` interface to connect to reverseproxies, loadbalancers, https endpoints etc. The charm should also implement a specific `wololodb` interface for clients who want to talk to the database. So we have two interface layers:

- `http` sends the hostname and the port and tells Juju that it can connect to other charms implementing the `http` interface.
- `wololodb` sends the hostname and port and tells Juju that it can connect to other charms implementing the `wololodb` interface.

Although both interfaces are identical, they need to be separate so that Juju knows that a `wololodb` client can only connect to a WololoDB server, not all HTTP servers.

This maps very cleanly to layers, the `wololodb` interface uses the `http` interface as base interface layer.

Whatever we come up with for reactive 2.0 should enable this kind of layered approach to interfaces.

# Totally epic

Juju should understand the interface inheritance. The HAProxy charm is a great use-case for this. The `reverseproxy` relationship has two "modes", the default mode compatible with the normal `http` interface, and an advanced mode where more info is shared over the relationship.

This would result in two interfaces

 - `http`
 - `reverseproxy` which `includes: ['http']`

When an operator connects a charm to haproxy, Juju knows that the `reverseproxy` relationship is preferred over the `http` relationship if both charms implement that interface.

issue_title: How to best add and act on custom metadata for hookspec_opts and hookimpl_opts
issue_body:
I'm looking to figure out the best way to deprecate a hook we have provided to end users and might be implemented in some plugins already. In a more general sense, I want to be able to add my own metadata to spec and impl opts and have ways of acting on that inside pluggy itself.

For the record, I have considered registering a try-first implementation or hook wrapper that raises the deprecation warning. And that's the easy answer, but I'd like to be able to include this information in documentation of the spec itself (potentially through a sphinx plugin or something similar) so inserting it into the `spec_opts` seemed like the wiser thing to do. From there, I'd also like to be able to act on it in the hook caller.

[However, I found this difficult](https://gist.github.com/justanr/6211ebda282f92803015ac7216eb5d90) mostly because of that static calls to `_HookCaller` that required copying large amounts of code into a subclass. Some of it could be cut down -- for example `_HookCaller.__call__` doesn't need to be overridden only `_HookCaller._hookexec` needs to be intercepted and wrapped.

I understand that I'm poking at things that are probably not intended to be poked at, or at least weren't considered open to being poked at. But is there potential for opening them up to poking?

# TransactionScope Support When Not Using EF

Using .NET Core 2.0

I wanted to use MVC for a new project but we don't want to use Enitity Framework since it will be based on an existing, large database used by other products.

However, when using our own business layer which uses TransactionScope, the .NET Core framework throws an exception.

I've since read TransactionScope is not yet supported.

This means we cannot possible use MVC with .NET Core 2.0 to build new products since data integrity is crucial

issue_title: Not possible to issue a certificate if Apache does not bind to all IPs
issue_body:
Firstly, very pleased with this project, and how far it has some in such a sort space of time!

On saying that, I do have an issue regarding Apache.  My current `ports.conf` is this, obviously replacing `(IP)` with my actual IP;
```
# If you just change the port or add more ports here, you will likely also
# have to change the VirtualHost statement in
# /etc/apache2/sites-enabled/000-default.conf

Listen (IP):80

<IfModule ssl_module>
        Listen (IP):443
</IfModule>

<IfModule mod_gnutls.c>
        Listen (IP):443
</IfModule>

# vim: syntax=apache ts=4 sw=4 sts=4 sr noet
```
However, this means certificate issuance fails with the following error;
```
IMPORTANT NOTES:
 - The following 'urn:acme:error:connection' errors were reported by
   the server:

   Domains: minersrealm.net, www.minersrealm.net
   Error: The server could not connect to the client for DV
```
...this means I am forced to use this configuration...;
```
# If you just change the port or add more ports here, you will likely also
# have to change the VirtualHost statement in
# /etc/apache2/sites-enabled/000-default.conf

Listen 80

<IfModule ssl_module>
        Listen 443
</IfModule>

<IfModule mod_gnutls.c>
        Listen 443
</IfModule>

# vim: syntax=apache ts=4 sw=4 sts=4 sr noet
```
...which then means Apache is binding to **all** IPs on the server, something which I do not want.  My main problem with this is I wish to automate the SSL renewal once every two months, something which I cannot do if I need to manually update the configuration everytime I wish to renew a certificate.

issue_body:
When I originally wrote the ISO 8601 parser, I misinterpreted the standard. From [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Times):

> Midnight is a special case and may be referred to as either "00:00" or "24:00". The notation "00:00" is used at the beginning of a calendar day and is the more frequently used. At the end of a day use "24:00". "2007-04-05T24:00" is the same instant as "2007-04-06T00:00" (see Combined date and time representations below).

But:

```python
>>> from dateutil.parser import isoparse
>>> isoparse("2007-04-05T24:00")
datetime.datetime(2007, 4, 5, 0, 0)
>>> isoparse("2007-04-06T00:00")
datetime.datetime(2007, 4, 6, 0, 0)
```

This is a bit tricky to handle because time and date parsing happen separately, so there's no obvious way to increment the date based on the value of the time until after it's parsed (at which point there is no difference between `00:00` and `24:00`).  I think the right solution is to allow [`_parse_isotime`](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/parser/isoparser.py#L318) to return `[24, 0, 0, 0, TZINFO]`, and handle that in [`parse_isotime`](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/parser/isoparser.py#L156) and [`parse_isodatetime`](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/parser/isoparser.py#L58) accordingly.

It's not clear to me whether the spec allows a bare time representation of `"24:00"` or if 24:00 is only valid in the context of a `datetime`. We should do whatever the spec says I think.

Call .disconnect() in the destructor of TelegramClient
issue_body:
Consider next code:

```python
import telethon

# NOTE: DON'T SHARE THESE VALUES!
TGAPP_API_ID =  #some id

Currently everything hangs if an exception happens, because TelegramClient uses threads.

issue_title: Feature request: Expose "tms" argument for TileLayer
issue_body:
The leaflet TileLayer tms option (http://leafletjs.com/reference-1.3.0.html#tilelayer-tms) is not currently exposed in the folium.TileLayer object. This makes it so that certain sources for tiles cannot be displayed in a folium Map (e.g., those created by gdal2tiles.py).

I think this would be a fairly straightforward fix by adding a tms argument to the TileLayer __init__ and then adding it to the _options_ variable. e.g.:

```python
    def __init__(self, tiles='OpenStreetMap', min_zoom=0, max_zoom=18,
                 max_native_zoom=None, attr=None, API_key=None,
                 detect_retina=False, name=None, overlay=False,
                 control=True, show=True, no_wrap=False, subdomains='abc', tms=False):
        self.tile_name = (name if name is not None else
                          ''.join(tiles.lower().strip().split()))
        super(TileLayer, self).__init__(name=self.tile_name, overlay=overlay,
                                        control=control, show=show)
        self._name = 'TileLayer'
        self._env = ENV

        options = {'minZoom': min_zoom,
                   'maxZoom': max_zoom,
                   'maxNativeZoom': max_native_zoom or max_zoom,
                   'noWrap': no_wrap,
                   'attribution': attr,
                   'subdomains': subdomains,
                   'detectRetina': detect_retina,
                   'tms': tms}
```

issue_title: Sync calls count_files / all_files without policies_manager, which causes incorrect local file system scanning
issue_body:
b2 sync calls count_files, which in turn calls all_files on the local folder.
However, it does not pass in the constructed policies_manager. This means it does not apply the regexs to exclude files/folders.

This causes incorrect file counting, and if you're relying on those directory exclusions to keep b2 from indexing problematic areas, can cause the system to do stuff you had been trying to keep it from doing.

In my case, there are certain linux folders which cause b2 to loop (eg there are some circular symlinks in /run).

I don't know the system well enough to say if there are consequences to this, but I did find a simple workaround of essentially piping policies_manager through sync_folders -> count_files -> all_files.

issue_title: Off by one error in raw_api._download_file_from_url
issue_body:
I keep thinking this must be an error on my part but I can't see how I'm wrong.

I'm calling the following function:

```python
def download_file_by_id(
        self, download_url, account_auth_token_or_none, file_id, download_dest, range_=None
    )
```

The [documentation](https://www.backblaze.com/b2/docs/b2_download_file_by_id.html) for downloading a file says about the range parameter:

> The value "bytes=0-99" selects bytes 0 through 99 (inclusive) of the file, so it will return the first 100 bytes

range_ is specified as a tuple (in _download_file_from_url)

```python
if range_ is not None:
    assert len(range_) == 2, range_
    assert (range_[0] + 0) <= (range_[1] + 0), range_  # not strings
    assert range_[0] >= 0, range_
    assert range_[1] >= 1, range_
    request_headers['Range'] = "bytes=%d-%d" % range_
```
So When I want the first 1048576 bytes and I request the range `(0, 1048576 - 1)`. The response header contains `'Content-Length', '1048576'` which is correct. But later on there is the following validation:

```python
desired_length = range_[1] - range_[0]
if bytes_read != desired_length:
    raise TruncatedOutput(bytes_read, desired_length)
```

But that fails with the message:
> TruncatedOutput: only 1048576 of 1048575 bytes read

I can only come to the conclusion that the desired length should have been:

```python
desired_length = range_[1] - range_[0] + 1
```

But I'm confused because I wouldn't expect me to be the only one to have this issue.

issue_title: Excluding fields during deserialization of object
issue_body:
The tuple notation seems to cause problems excluding fields during the serialization process. That documentation states that the following should work:

``` python
summary_schema = UserSchema(exclude=('name'))
summary_schema.dump(user)
# {"email": "monty@python.org", ...}
```

Unfortunately, specifying a tuple does not work if it only contains one element (in this case `name`). What works is to simply pass a list instead of a tuple, like this:

``` python
summary_schema = UserSchema(exclude=['name'])
summary_schema.dump(user)
# {"email": "monty@python.org", ...}
```
I think the documentation should be adapted to avoid confusion.

issue_title: sqlalchemy marshmallow avoid loading data into session
issue_body:
Is there a way to avoid inserting the data into session while using Marshmallow - sqlalchemy .load()

Because we tried to manage the objects by ourself (adding them into session if required) . Will add into the session if required , but for only validation I need to use load () method which is provided by marshmallow-sqlalchemy

Load Code for marshmallow-sqlalchemy

def load(self, data, session=None, instance=None, *args, **kwargs):
        """Deserialize data to internal representation.
        :param session: Optional SQLAlchemy session.
        :param instance: Optional existing instance to modify.
        """
        self.session = session or self.session
        self.instance = instance or self.instance
        if not self.session:
            raise ValueError('Deserialization requires a session')

Hi, I use webargs to handle my request arguments, I set schema `only` and `exclude` as an arguments.

```
myargs = {
    'only': fields.DelimitedList(fields.Str(), missing=None),
    'exclude': fields.DelimitedList(fields.Str(), missing=None)
}
@use_args(myargs, locations=['query'])
def get(self, args):
    schema = MySchema(many=True, only=args['only'], exclude=args['exclude'])
```

since by default `only` and `exclude` is set as None, if  `exclude` is None in my request, it returns 

`TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable`

currently I fixed it with a simple conditional in line 
https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/schema.py#L633 

```
if self.exclude:
            excludes = set(self.opts.exclude) | set(self.exclude)
            if excludes:
                # Apply the exclude option to nested fields.
                self.__apply_nested_option('exclude', excludes)
```
and line https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/schema.py#L677

Thx

If we could specify the entry point the merging tools would be a working solution!

The 400-level errors are responding with a JSON array containing error objects, but the JSON API spec [requires](http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-top-level) a top-level `error` field.

##### To reproduce

1. Use `serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)` in an API view
2. Send invalid JSON from the client to *any* API endpoint

    Example:
    ```js
    ajax({
      url: '/my/api',
      contentType: 'application/vnd.api+json',
      type: 'POST',
      data: 'not JSON'
    });
    ```

    Expected response:
    ```json
    {
      "errors": [
        {
          "status": "400",
          "source": {"pointer": "/data"},
          "detail": "JSON parse error - No JSON object could be decoded"
        }
      ]
    }
    ```

    Actual response:    
    ```json
      [
        {
          "status": "400",
          "source": {"pointer": "/data"},

The former gets called, but the latter does not. DRF seems to have taken over, responding with this project's `exception_handler` as-is.

djangorestframework==3.3.3

It would be a great feature to have some integration with hyperparameter optimization tools like
[hyperopt](https://github.com/hyperopt/hyperopt), [spearmint](https://github.com/HIPS/Spearmint), [SMAC](http://www.cs.ubc.ca/labs/beta/Projects/SMAC), [Whetlab](https://www.whetlab.com/), [apsis](https://github.com/FrederikDiehl/apsis), [BayesOpt](http://rmcantin.bitbucket.org/html/), ...

You would add a search space definition to an experiment, which would specify the domain for each configuration variable that should be searched. And then we could provide a simple interface to convert these definitions into the formats needed by the various hyperparameter optimizers and ask them to suggest a new run. 
 
Seach-Space Definition Languages
--------------------------------------------
Here is a simple example of how [Whetlab does that](https://www.whetlab.com/docs/python-tutorial/#setting-up-a-whetlab-experiment):

```python
space = { 
    'C':{'min':0.01, 'max':1000.0,'type':'float'},
    'degree':{'min':1, 'max':5,'type':'integer'}}
```

The same space defined in hyperopt:
```python
from hyperopt import hp
space = {
    'C': hp.lognormal('C', 0.01, 1000.),
    'degree': hp.randint('degree', 5) + 1}
```

In spearmint the corresponding region from the config.json file would be:
``` json
"variables": {
    "C" : {"type" : "FLOAT", "size" : 1, "min" : 0.01, "max" : 1000},
    "degree" : {"type" : "INT", "size" : 1, "min" : 1,  "max" : 5}
}
```

In SMAC the parameters have a range and a default value (see [manual](http://www.cs.ubc.ca/labs/beta/Projects/SMAC/v2.08.00/manual.pdf) page 12):
```
C [0.01, 1000.] [1.]
degree [1, 5] [5]i
```

There is also the [searchspaces project](https://github.com/hyperopt/searchspaces/) which tries to provide a common language for specifying searchspaces. Unfortunately it seems to be abandoned. (But here is a [description](https://docs.google.com/viewer?a=v&pid=sites&srcid=ZGVmYXVsdGRvbWFpbnxhdXRvbWx3c2ljbWwxNHxneDo2NWNkNWZhYWZhMTQ1N2Ux) of what they were planning). In their language the example would have looked somthing like this:
```python
from searchspaces import variable
space = {
    'C': variable('C', minimum=0.01, maximum=1000., type=float),
    'degree': variable('degree', minimum=1, maximum=5, type=int),
}
```

API Ideas
------------
So for Sacred the nicest way would be to have an `@ex.search_space` decorator that produces a special ConfigScope for searchspaces. Something along these lines:
``` python
from sacred import search_space as sp

@ex.search_space
def my_random_search_space():
    C = sp.lognormal(0.01, 1000.)
    degree = sp.integer(1, 5)
```
The most amazing would be to support operations with literals like hyperopt does:
``` python
@ex.search_space
def my_random_search_space():
    foo = 1 - sp.lognormal(0.01, 1.)
    bar = (sp.integer(1, 5) * 3) + 1
```
But then we need to take extra precautions not to loose compatibility with tools like spearmint or whetlab.

One idea could be to have the search-space work in three stages:
  1. build an evaluation tree based on the written expressions, and collect all the non-constant nodes (*search nodes*)
  2. hand the list of search-nodes to a hyperparameter optimization algorithm and get the results
  3. plug the results back in and evaluate the graph

This might be a lot of work, but would provide a very powerful interface and should be compatible with most hyperparameter optimization tools. (It seems that this was also the basic idea behind  [searchspaces](https://github.com/hyperopt/searchspaces/))

For using SMAC we would need a default value for each entry, but it seems tedious to provide one by hand. We could have a default-default (like always the mean/median of the searchspace) and make it possible to set manually if needed.

In at least one place we locally create and populate instances of Mopidy's models ourselves. Previously this idea has led to bugs when we then tried to pass these models back to Mopidy, at which point they turned out to invalid/wrong format/junk. 

I think we now only pass `Ref`s to Mopidy so bugs like this are hopefully gone. However, I can't think of any good reasons why we'd need to create and populate these models ourselves locally and if it's no longer necessary (since we don't use them externally) we should aim to entirely remove this practice.

issue_title: Fractional powers and even roots yield negative numbers
issue_body:
Running Z3 on the following sequence of propositions

`(declare-const x Real)`
`(assert (= 1 (^ x (/ 1 2))))`
`(check-sat-using qfnra-nlsat)`
`(get-model)`
`(eval (= 1 (^ x (/ 1 2))))`

produces

`sat`
`(model`
`    (define-fun x () Real`
`        (- 1.0)) `
`)`
`Z3(5, 25): ERROR: even root of negative number is not real`

Note that the final line simply evaluates the equation from line 2 on the proposed solution for x, so Z3 seems to contradict itself.

issue_title: How to query models for boxed objectives?
issue_body:
Is there a way to query z3 so I can get the satisfying models for a specific objective in case I have boxed (or pareto) objectives?

This is what I'm trying:

```
(declare-fun a () Int)

(assert (>  a  2))
(assert (<= a 10))

(minimize (+ (* 4 a) 2))
(maximize (+ 5 (* 2 a)))

(set-option :opt.priority box)

(check-sat)
(get-value (a))
```

This returns:

```
sat
(objectives
 ((+ (* 4 a) 2) 14)
 ((+ 5 (* 2 a)) 25)
)
((a 3))
```

I interpret this as the first goal `4a+2` optimized to a minimal value of `14` under the assignment `a=3`; which is nicely returned by the `(get-value (a))` call.

Similarly, `2a+5` has been maximized at value `25`, when `a=10`.

But, how do I query z3 for the `a=10` value, i.e., the assignment for the second objective?

In general, if I have `box` objectives, which goal does `get-value` calls refer to? It seems the answer is the very first goal from my experiments: Is that always true? How do I access the values for other goals? (Or better yet, is there a way to specify which objective `get-value` should refer to?)

I also have a very similar question for `pareto` optimization: How do I obtain values of model values for different objective values?

Hi everyone!

Just came across an example which seems to be "difficult" for Z3 to handle:

The following returns unknown:
```
(declare-const i_0 Real)
(declare-const i_1 Real)

(declare-const k_0 Real)

(declare-const j_0 String)
(declare-const j_1 String)

(assert (and
          (>= i_0 0.0)
          (= i_1 (* i_0 k_0))

	  (= "0" j_0)
          (= j_0 j_1)
        )
)

(assert (not
          (and
            (>= i_1 0.0)
            (= "0" j_1)
          )
        )
)

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

This also returns unknown:
```
(declare-const i_0 Real)
(declare-const i_1 Real)

(declare-const k_0 Real)

(declare-const j_0 Int)
(declare-const j_1 Int)

(assert (and
          (>= i_0 0.0)
          (= i_1 (* i_0 k_0))

	  (= 0 j_0)
          (= j_0 j_1)
        )
)

(assert (not
          (and
            (>= i_1 0.0)
            (= 0 j_1)
          )
        )
)

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

While this returns sat:
```
(declare-const i_0 Real)
(declare-const i_1 Real)

(declare-const k_0 Real)

(declare-const j_0 Real)
(declare-const j_1 Real)

(assert (and
          (>= i_0 0.0)
          (= i_1 (* i_0 k_0))

	  (= 0 j_0)
          (= j_0 j_1)
        )
)

(assert (not
          (and
            (>= i_1 0.0)
            (= 0 j_1)
          )
        )
)

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
With model:
```
i_0: 1.0
i_1: - 1.0
k_0: - 1.0
j_0: 0.0
j_1: 0.0
```

The differences between the examples is the type of `j_0` and `j_1` which is either String, Int or Real. Only when it matches the type of `i_0` and `i_1` as a Real is it able to find the satisfying model. To me this is confusing as the variables for `i_*` and `j_*`  do not share any relation and the model does not really changes.

I strongly suspect this is a bug, hence the report. If I am doing something wrong, please tell me!

To help the most users understand how to get their data into NWB, I propose that we target a handful of dominant preprocessing pipelines (that is, spike sorting & calcium segmentation/ trace extraction) & give examples of getting that data into NWB.

- One of the pain points of getting data into NWB is accounting for provenance information related to a processing pipeline (i.e. saving the ROI and neuropil subtraction in addition to the calcium traces)
- While we won't promise a script that does a conversion, this should help get users that use these existing processing pipelines 90% of the way there.
- Each of these pieces of software outputs a fairly standardized set of files.

I'd like to brainstorm and identify a handful of target "sources" for ingest into NWB.

We can then either write examples ourselves or solicit the developers of the respective tools to contribute.

My initial brainstorm:

**kilosort / phy TemplateGUI**
This is the dominant spike sorting software for dense ephys recordings. 
- **modality**: ephys
- **output files**: npy & tsv
- **docs on outputs**: http://phy-contrib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/template-gui/#analysis
- **developers**: @kwikteam

**suite2p**
AFAIK, this is the dominant segmentation and trace extraction processing software around right now.
- **modality**: 2P
- **output files**: mat
- **docs on outputs**: https://github.com/cortex-lab/Suite2P#iii-outputs
- **developers**: @marius10p

## Feature Request

Codecov has a feature which is used to set a coverage threshold for PRs. Here are the advantages and disadvantages for this feature:

### Pros
1) This will ensure PR's can only be merged with "some" testing. "some" is dependent on how we set up the threshold. If we set it up to 100%, that means every line that is newly added needs to be covered before getting merged. I recommend setting it to something like 80% just to make sure we maintain a high quality in our PRs.
2) If a feature is added or bug fixed and there are tests associated with the feature/bug fix, it is much more easier to reason about the PR for the reviewer. 
3) This will ensure we don't drop our coverage significantly while implementing new features. 
### Cons
1) WE HAVE TO WRITE TESTS.

I am more than happy to implement it if there are no objections.
Read the docs is not building again.

issue_title: Remoting list subclasses fail (or really any class that has built-in so unhashable methods)
issue_body:
First of all, thanks very much for this library.  It's really useful and works nicely most of the time.  However, I did encounter an issue when trying to expose a list subclass as a zeroservice.  The initial exception was:

    File "/Users/prak/code/venv/twola/lib/python2.7/site-packages/zerorpc/core.py", line 109, in _inject_builtins
    self._methods['_zerorpc_list'] = lambda: [m for m in self._methods
    TypeError: list indices must be integers, not str

However, this was just a symptom caused by the fact that ServerBase._filter_methods (core.py:71) seems to try and check if its methods argument is a dict by seeing if it has a __getitem__ method.  I was able to get by this explicitly doing isinstance(dict).

Next there was an issue with the dict comprehension in the return statement (core.py:76) in that checking whether a method is "not in server_methods" requires that thing to be hashable which means built in functions break it.  A fix is to replace the return call with:

        return dict((k, getattr(methods, k))
                for k in dir(methods)
                if callable(getattr(methods, k))
                and not k.startswith('_')
                and (inspect.isbuiltin(getattr(methods, k)) or
                     getattr(methods, k) not in server_methods)
                )

which requires importing the inspect module.  I'm not sure if this solution is worthwhile though because remoting dict subclasses will remain an issue and it really seems like it's the multiple calls to ServerBase._filter_methods from zerorpc.Server that are a bit awkward.

A test to reproduce the error (I put it in test_server.py):

    def test_server_can_wrap_list():
        endpoint = random_ipc_endpoint()
    
        l = list()
        srv = zerorpc.Server(list)
        srv.bind(endpoint)

The test is pretty weak but I'm not familiar enough with the codebase yet to do much more. If you guys are ok with this solution, I'd be happy to go through the fork, commit, pull request process.

issue_title: Condor() backend fails trying to copy file to node
issue_body:
When I submit a ganga job via the `Condor()` backend, I get the following error

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/var/lib/condor/execute/dir_21888/condor_exec.exe", line 161, in <module>
    shutil.copy(inFile, os.path.join(os.getcwd(), os.path.basename(inFile)))
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/shutil.py", line 133, in copy
    copyfile(src, dst)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/shutil.py", line 83, in copyfile
    raise Error("`%s` and `%s` are the same file" % (src, dst))
shutil.Error: `ntuple_maker_mc_12103091_2011.py` and `/var/lib/condor/execute/dir_21888/ntuple_maker_mc_12103091_2011.py` are the same file
```

where `ntuple_maker_mc_12103091_2011.py` is my DaVinci submission script.

I believe the offending lines are: 
* https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/47825403bdb9f6d6123d1b6745751f0711318d15/python/GangaCore/Lib/Condor/Condor.py#L288-L290
* https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/47825403bdb9f6d6123d1b6745751f0711318d15/python/GangaCore/Lib/Condor/Condor.py#L315-L319
such that `shutil.copy` effectively ends up trying to copy the file over itself.

issue_title: Running nightly tests against all targets
issue_body:
Hi all,

I've just hit yet another bug where my code ran fine on my machine but failed in testing due to a different configuration.
As a small step to try and avoid problems like this creeping in I propose a minor change to the nightly script to run both the `Ganga/test` and `GangaPlugin/test` directories against the given environment and configuration of each plugin.

Is there a solid reason why we wouldn't expect all of the Ganga tests to pass for all users?

Rob

issue_title: The kinto/kinto-server Docker image doesn't contain the kinto-admin
issue_body: Apparently the kinto-server docker image doesn't embedded the kinto-admin files.
[Selecting fields](http://kinto.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/1.x/selecting_fields.html) doesn't work with other verbs rather than GET. Should it? The documentation says nothing about it.

Now that `cliquet` is in `kinto.core`, some naming is confusion.

In the core, resources are called collections, and objects are called records.

But, in Kinto, collections and records are instantiation of resources!

I suggest some renaming in kinto.core: 
- `collection` to `resource`
- `record` to `object`
- `collection_id` to `resource_name`
- `record_id` to `object_id`

That represents quite some work, but reducing confusion is always worth it.

issue_title: [AMBER] Memory footprint goes on increasing: pytraj traj.iterchunk used, distances between bonds calculation
issue_body:
from AMBER mailing list, by SHAILESH KUMAR

------
Dear all,

I am trying to process a big trajectory which can not fit in memory my
computer. So, I tried using Iterating over the full trajectory using
iterchunk method in pytraj. In each iteration bond lengths are calculated
for the frames in chunk, and can be written to a file (which currently is
disable for memory profiling purpose)  for further analysis because memory
footprint of the process keeps on growing.

Its pseudo code can be as follows:

```python
traj = ptraj.iterload(trajfile, prmtopfile, frame_slice=slice_info)

for chunk in traj.iterchunk(chunk_size, start=0, start=-1):
     bnd_vals = pt.distance(chunk, bnd_list, dtype='ndarray')
     # do process bnd_vals() ## curently disabled
     gc.collect()
```

But on memory profiling it was observed that memory keeps on increasing in
ievery iteration of chunks. Which indicates memory leak in
bnd_vals = pt.distance(chunk, bnd_list, dtype='ndarray')
but why is not clear to me. May be I am doing something wrong and not able
to spot it, or there is memory leak somewhere (may be in api).


Now I ask for help, if any one can help me to sort it out, it would be a
great favor. For reproduciblity i am attaching simplified code, with
necessary input files except trajectory, i can share it using dropbox when
needed. This test dataset corresponds to a small molecule. Actual problem
is to do similar for actual protein molecules.

Hello, 

I am using 64-bit Anaconda version 4.3.0 with Python 3.6 and PyCharm EDU 3.5 on Windows 8.1. I have tried installing PyWavelets both from Anaconda command prompt and PyCharm packages ( using anaconda and python interpreters). I have used following commands on anaconda command prompt.

issue_title: Best way to implement rate limiting?
issue_body:
Testing out one of the [examples](https://github.com/meraki-analytics/cassiopeia/blob/master/examples/match.py):


`    


    champion_id_to_name_mapping = {champion.id: champion.name for champion in cass.get_champions(region=region)}
    played_champions = Counter()
    for match in match_history:
        champion_id = match.participants[summoner.name].champion.id
        champion_name = champion_id_to_name_mapping[champion_id]
        played_champions[champion_name] += 1


Printing the first few elements looked like this:
Leona  855
Caitlyn  27
Vladimir  26
Braum  23
Nocturne  22

Doing this a for a few other users quickly got my api key blacklisted. Did this really make over a thousand api calls? Is there a way I can get many many match histories in a few calls? or is every match always going to be a full api call? Right now I added another counter to stop after 50 match histories but this seems like a futile solution, since I am still making 50 calls for one users request. I don't really want to do a massive database system of match histories like how op.gg or other sites might do for match histories.

I'm running into an issue with the ranked 3v3 queues. It looks to me like the current queue and the "depreciated" queue are equal.

```
>>> cass.Queue.depreciated_ranked_flex_threes == cass.Queue.ranked_flex_threes
True
```

This causes problems with evaluation of things like, "Which queue is this league for?". For example, when looking at the league info for my summoner--

```
>>> cass.Summoner(name="Kihashi", region="NA").leagues[0].queue
<Queue.depreciated_ranked_flex_threes: 'RANKED_FLEX_TT'>
```

Since this is data for the current season, I would not expect the output to be the "depreciated" queue. I would expect it to be `Queue.ranked_flex_threes`.

Is this expected behavior or is this just a result of the enum being the same value?

(Also, I'm a little confused by the used of "depreciated" vs "deprecated").

issue_title: Cannot implement optional argument for queries
Does relay.NodeField support many args more than "id" ?
issue_body:
Hi, guys

In `graphene/relay/fields.py` : 

```python
class NodeField(Field):
    # ...
    def id_fetcher(self, global_id, info):
        # ...
        return object_type.get_node(_id, info)

    def resolver(self, instance, args, info):
        global_id = args.get('id')
        return self.id_fetcher(global_id, info)
```

This define a `get_node` strictly, just global id could be used as arg. 

There is an example about other scene: 

[http://graphql-swapi.parseapp.com](http://graphql-swapi.parseapp.com/?query={%0A%20%20film%28filmID%3A%20%223%22%29%20{%0A%20%20%20%20id%0A%20%20%20%20title%0A%20%20}%0A%20%20allFilms%20{%0A%20%20%20%20edges%20{%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20node%20{%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20id%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20title%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20}%0A%20%20%20%20}%0A%20%20}%0A}%0A)

They define a more useful interface: 

```
film(id: ID, filmID: ID): Film
person(id: ID, personID: ID): Person
planet(id: ID, planetID: ID): Planet
species(id: ID, speciesID: ID): Species
starship(id: ID, starshipID: ID): Starship
vehicle(id: ID, vehicleID: ID): Vehicle
node(id: ID!): Node
```

It has a 'XXXID' arg in every node field. This is just what I wanted. It would be useful where a `NodeField` can queried by other args not just id.

Any help would be appreciated.
Thanks!

Refs:

- [Relay Global Object Identification Specification](https://facebook.github.io/relay/graphql/objectidentification.htm)

In our project, we commit a graphql schema to our repo.  This is useful for running demos, tests and mocking data.

However, now we also want to be able to ensure that the schema is kept up-to-date with a CI check. issue_body:
Currently there are two problems about OpenThread implementation that would affect this case:
1. Child ID Response message does not include Active Timestamp Tlv if child has a same value with its parent. It's compatible with spec requirement. However, harness will check this filed in Child ID Response massage. 
(If Active Timestamp Tlv and Pending Timestamp Tlv would not bring negative impact, from testing purpose, maybe we can add them back)
 
2. For now, Pending Dataset will only include TLVs carried from MGMT_PENDING_SET command. However, Harness will check whether or not it also contains those TLVs in Active Dataset as well such as security policy tlv, PSKc tlv, channelMask tlv.... (during initialization Harness configures these Tlvs via THCI, so we can see that Active Dataset includes all TLVs.)

Spec states that
"The Pending Operational Dataset contains all the parameters from the Active Operational Dataset, with the addition of Delay timer and pending timestamp".
Maybe we can append these TLVs from Active Dataset to Pending Dataset as well. Please let me know your advice. Thanks.

issue_title: xlrd: in2csv fails to load codepage 21010 xls files
issue_body:
This is a common issue in CSV parsers/Excel exporters apparently: 

- https://github.com/tafia/calamine/pull/86 "Treat codepage 21010 as codepage 1200"
- https://github.com/SheetJS/js-xls/issues/44 "xls: error parsing bad.xls: Error: Unrecognized CP: 21010"
- https://github.com/PHPOffice/PHPExcel/issues/396 "Unknown codepage: 21010"

It seems to be generated from MacOS versions of Excel. 

Basically, when encountering codepage 21010 it should interpret it as codepage 1200 (AKA UTF-16le).

Ideally this would be handled programmatically. However, even passing in `--encoding utf-16le` (or other variations) seem to have no effect on `in2csv`, so it might be ignoring the encoding argument?

Hello All, 

I'd like to help Dask-ML develop a roadmap, and @TomAugspurger requested that I open an issue here to kickstart the discussion. 

For those of you unfamiliar, a roadmap is a listing of near- and long-term goals that the project has, but which have yet to be implemented. These goals are generally larger than single pull request. "Add GPU Support" or "Port to Python 3" are examples of some roadmap items a project might have. However, goals can also include activities that seem small and mundane, but are critical to the project, such as "Improve Documentation" or "Achieve 100% Test Coverage."

**The purpose of this issue is so that roadmap items can be listed and commented on in the discussion.** Once we have a reasonable sense of what folks would like to see on the roadmap, the contents should make its way to the website/docs in some fashion. It is also a good idea if it ends up being listed in priority order, so that higher priority items are closer to the top. 

Some good example of project roadmaps are:

* [SymPy Roadmap](http://sympy.org/en/roadmap.html)
* [Bokeh Roadmap](https://bokehplots.com/pages/roadmap.html)

A decent example of a project roadmap is [Spyder's Roadmap](https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder/wiki/Roadmap) which looks more like a timeline with milestones.

Roadmaps serve a joint purpose:

* They help inform and attract potentail funders, and some funders require roadmap documents
* They help attract contributors, as developers are more likely to jump in an help an establish project than start a new project to scratch their itch.

**TL;DR:** Roadmaps communicate a project's intentions. It would be a good idea for Dask-ML to have one, and I am happy to help facilitate!

So where would you like to see Dask-ML go?

This is a mirror issue to https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/3589

issue_title: What Django versions should Anymail support?
issue_body:
Right now, Anymail is only building on Django 1.8 and 1.9.

Most of the code (from Djrill) is still compatible all the way back to Django 1.4. There are a few tests that are using some newer timezone pieces.

If it's important for people migrating from Djrill, it wouldn't be too hard to maintain compatibility with older Django versions.

But if everyone's already on Django 1.8 or later, that effort could probably be better spent elsewhere.

issue_title: Avoid saving the entire item in memory for Items returned by filesystem storage
issue_labels: [{'id': 90006582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/cat:%20performance', 'name': 'cat: performance', 'color': '5319e7', 'default': False}, {'id': 529561413, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/lv:%20hard', 'name': 'lv: hard', 'color': 'fbca04', 'default': False}, {'id': 336204756, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/ready', 'name': 'ready', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}, {'id': 80748618, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'type: enhancement', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
During sync, all items that need to be copied from A to B are fetched from A at once. This alone makes RAM consumption of synchronization `O(n)` for initial sync, though it already is for other reasons (e.g. #546 not being implemented). I haven't found a fix for that, and I actually believe that it can be proven that the current sync semantics can't be done more efficiently wrt RAM consumption than in `O(n)` (note though that `O` is an upper bound, assuming the absolute worst case scenario that is the initial sync).

However, we can at least reduce the constant factor by a lot. Currently `Item` is basically a wrapper around a string that contains the entire item content. This is not very memory-efficient, and pretty unnecessary for item content that is fetched from the local disk.

What I want to have is a subclass of `Item` that wraps a file on the local FS:

* It doesn't store the full item content, only the filepath and the etag, which are passed via the constructor.
* When accessing `raw`, it opens the file, **validates the etag** against the one it has stored (!!) reads the entire content, closes and returns the content.
* `uid` is cached, as is `ident`, but that part can just be inherited from the superclass

etag (=mtime) validation is important because I'm concerned about other programs modifying the file while synchronization is happening. It isn't foolproof but lowers the chance by a lot.

That new superclass is then used by `filesystem` storage's `get` method. However, that behavior should possibly be configurable to accomodate for situations where the old behavior is better (the underlying FS is slow, or badly cached). That means a new parameter for the `filesystem` storage.

issue_title: Print deprecation warning on experimental systems in certbot-auto
issue_labels: [{'id': 276243379, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20certbot-auto', 'name': 'area: certbot-auto', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 276667632, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20pkging', 'name': 'area: pkging', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 281347824, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
We have a number of systems where we have "experimental" bootstrap support but we have OS packages available. We should print a deprecation warning for these systems telling users that support is going away including how to install the OS packages. We will be removing support completely for these systems in a few months. The current experimental systems are:

* Mageia
* Manjaro 
* Gentoo
* FreeBSD
* macOS
* Amazon Linux
* Joyent SmartOS Zone

At the very least, Gentoo, FreeBSD, and macOS have other options but we should check out other systems as well.

issue_title: Too low settle_timeout on channel open causes 500 in Rest-API
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Calling `PUT /api/1/channels` with a `settle_timeout` < `DEFAULT_SETTLE_TIMEOUT` causes `500 Internal Server Error`. The reason is that `RaidenAPI.open()` raises a `ValueError` in this case which isn't handled in `RestAPI.open()`.

## Solution

Handle `ValueError` in `RestAPI.open()` and return a descriptive error message.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Handle `ValueError` in `RestAPI.open()` and return a descriptive error message.

issue_title: Define the maximum number of pending transfers
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Define the upper limit for the merkle tree size, since the proof is iterate in the `unlock` function.

## Solution

Find what is the maximum length of the merkle proof (thus also the merkle tree height) that can be unlocked with the available gas limit (#438).

## Tasklist

- [ ] Write a utility to find the largest tree height, i.e. increase the merkle tree height by one until unlock fails.
- [ ] Define this value as a `MAXIMUM_PENDING_TRANSFERS` constant (pending transfers is easier to reason about and to explain to the user in respect to the proof length or tree height).
- [ ] Use this maximum height to reject new mediated transfers.
- [ ] Write a test that checks the maximum height is enforced.
- [ ] Write a test to verify the constant has a sane value.

issue_title: Change cancel icon to start after job canceled

Right now tasks are independent entities. It would be good to store step name or ID in the task file so that we know what these tasks belong to. issue_title: -W (no wait) test fail for no obvious reason.
issue_body:
As show in [this travis test](https://travis-ci.org/vatlab/SoS/jobs/287925981), the `test_task.py TestTask.testKillAndPurge` test failed because `sos` command hang after the jobs are submitted. I cannot reproduce this problem on mac, on linux, even on a [TravisCI virtual machine](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/21053657/how-to-run-travis-ci-locally). My suspicion is that the travis server has perhaps only 2 cores so the extra task management thread somehow stopped the execution of a worker.

I am disabling this test for now but I will keep this ticket open and check it when we touch this part of code later.

SoS makes it easy to submit tasks to HPC but it still requires the HPC to have the software installed. The idea (actually current implementation) of sos running docker container is practical for workstations but not for HPC because of security concerns. [Singularity](http://singularity.lbl.gov/) is a container solution for HPC as demonstrated by [its paper](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article/file?type=printable&id=10.1371/journal.pone.0177459) and had been adopted by universities.

Without checking the actual capacity of singularity, on the surface it looks like we could wrap our tasks as singularity apps and execute them on HPC and free users the need to install software there. This should definitely be something we should try, which can make sos an even better platform for cross-platform computation.
