issue_title: [webui] Generating a new task timeline requires moving the slider.
issue_body:
It should be sufficient to click "View task timeline" again. But it seems like you have to move the slider.

Moving the slider is probably triggering queries to the global state API, which is unnecessary (in the case of the timeline).

issue_title: Python3 2x slower than Python2 for some operations
issue_body:
<!--
General questions should be asked on the mailing list ray-dev@googlegroups.com.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.
-->

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: OS X
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: source
- **Python version**: Python 2.7 & Python 3.6

### Describe the problem
I am seeing a slowdown of 2x when I run things on Python3 vs Python2. The slowdown doesn't look to be linked to creating actors, but rather in executing certain types of tasks.

Code to reproduce is below.
`python2 time: 4.214621067047119`
`python3 time: 9.676268259005155`

### Source code / logs

```python
import ray
ray.init()

@ray.remote
class Actor(object):
    def __init__(self, rows={"Item1": "Value1", "Item2": "Value2"}):
        self.rows = rows
    def split(self):
        half = int(len(self.rows)/2)
        items = list(self.rows.items())
        items.sort()
        second_dict = dict(items[half:])
        self.rows = dict(items[:half])
        return second_dict
    def get_rows(self):
        return self.rows

def f():
    actor = Actor.remote()
    actor2 = Actor.remote(actor.split.remote())
    print(ray.get(actor2.get_rows.remote()))
    print(ray.get(actor.get_rows.remote()))

import timeit
timeit.timeit(f, number=10)
```

To reproduce the problem, do the following

```python
import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def f(x):
    return 1

x = np.zeros(1000)

for _ in range(100000): f.remote(x)
```

Now the non-primary Redis shard should be using a decent amount of memory. Check how much it is using with `ps aux | grep "redis-server "`.

Attach a Redis client to it with

```
redis-cli -p <PORT>
```

You can get the port from the output of `ray.init()` or from `ps aux | grep "redis-server "`.

In the Redis client, do `FLUSHALL` to flush all of the keys.

Check how much memory the Redis server is using. It's still using the same amount.

issue_title: Remote function cannot recursively call itself.
issue_body:
Consider the following script.

```python
import ray

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def f():
  return ray.get(f.remote())

f.remote()
```

When `f.remote()` is called, the following error is printed.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-3-6f5c275d0ce3>", line 3, in f
NameError: name 'f' is not defined
```

This worked at some point but seems to have gotten broken. We weren't testing recursively defining remote functions because defining remote functions in this manner inside of the Python unit testing framework didn't work. However, we should add tests for this behavior and just not use the unit testing framework for that part.

### Checklist

- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [x] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [x] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description

Many times when I'm downloading a livestream I left it running without supervision. At times, livestream download suddenly "ends" thinking that livestream is already over when it's not, for some reason.

So I'm requesting a feature to be able to automatically restart and retry a livestream download a set number of times with a set duration of time inbetween each tries, once it detects that livestream have "ended". 

This is catastrophic when you're archiving a critical livestream, as you can be missing a huge chunk of time from the recording.

Please do consider my suggestion!

issue_title: keyboard interrupt should exit with code 130
issue_body:
this is just a convention, 

https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/223189/what-does-exit-code-130-mean-for-postgres-command
 
 - if the process exited with exit(n) or return n from main(): the lower 8 bits of n (n & 0xFF).
 - if the process was killed by signal n: n + 128.

which can make streamlink more friendly when using within script.

issue_body:
Hello
Thanks for parallel-ssh, I think it's going to be really useful for me!

I hit a problem which seems to be caused by me doing `import paramiko` before doing `import pssh`.

You can reproduce simply by adding `import paramiko` to the top of `examples/parallel_commands.py`, it goes from working as expected to failing with this kind of error:

```
{'localhost': 
	host=localhost
	cmd=<Greenlet at 0x7f0d14041d70>
	channel=None
	stdout=None
	stderr=None
	stdin=None
	exception=("General SSH error - Error reading SSH protocol banner('This operation would block forever', <Hub at 0x7f0d14041e10 epoll pending=0 ref=0 fileno=17>)", 'localhost', 22)
}
```

If `paramiko` is imported after `pssh` then there's no issue.

I can reproduce with Python 2.7 and Python 3.4, commit f64ad595f770e957f1f0b1 of parallel-ssh, and Paramiko 2.1.2. The OS I'm using is CentOS 7.

The reason I added the `import paramiko` line was to load an SSH key following the example here: https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/blob/master/pssh/pssh_client.py#L262

issue_title: Dataset add should not attempt to auto-match lineage datasets if they exist in db
issue_body:
When adding datasets to the database `datacube dataset add` does roughly following

- For every input dataset
   1. Auto-match dataset to a product (fail if not found)
   2. For every dataset in the lineage of an input dataset
         1. Auto-match dataset to the product (fail if not found)

If you are indexing derived dataset with lineage that already exists in the database, then second auto-match is not necessary, those datasets are already in the database, and are already associated to a product. 

This behaviour makes `--dtype` option behave in a way that most users won't expect, when indexing datasets with lineage. Here is a help string for this option:

```
  -t, --dtype TEXT                Product to be associated with the datasets
```

Several problems already

1. Using `dtype` name that is highly associated with `numpy.dtype` in the target community, but is not *that kind of dtype*
2. Actually means `dataset-type` == `product`
3. Inconsistent naming on one line: *"dataset type"* on a command line, *"product"* in the help string
4. You can actually specify multiple products, help doesn't make this clear

What this option really is: 

> Restrain auto-matching to a subset of products from the database (you can supply several on a command line), auto-matching is applied to the new datasets listed on the command line and any lineage datasets referenced in the metadata.

### User experience scenario

1. Created some new datasets using stats tool or similar (so they have lineage data)
2. Added product definition for my new datasets
3. Tried running with default options and was told that my datasets could match to several products in the DB and so no indexing can proceed
   - `datacube product add` will not warn you when adding products with "too similar" metadata
4. No worries I can specify product with this weirdly named `--dtype` option
5. Try running `datacube dataset add -t my_new_product my_new_dataset.nc`, still get an error

But I just told you which product to use!!?

The problem is that now that you have restrained set of products to match against to just one product you care about, you can't auto-match any of the lineage products at all. 

But you should not need to, those datasets are already in the DB, no need to auto-match them in this case. Sure you still need to auto-match IF and ONLY IF lineage datasets are not in the DB already.

### Conclusion

Combination of poor documentation and decision to auto-match before checking DB makes `--dtype` option unusable to anyone but those who are ultimately familiar with the implementation of `datacube dataset add` command.

issue_title: Time range strings behave differently between datacube and xarray
issue_body:
When specifying a time range in a datacube:

is interpreted to be:

i.e. values round out
Ideally they would behave the same.

issue_title: Invalid target compile option for MSVC build
issue_body:
https://github.com/onnx/onnx/commit/8bbeb2ae453a113f6557afe5048d428a69af8bbb had these changes

-if (WIN32)
-    # parallel build
-    add_compile_options(/MP)
**-    add_compile_options(/WX)**
-    set(CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS "${CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS} /WX")
-    set(CMAKE_STATIC_LINKER_FLAGS "${CMAKE_STATIC_LINKER_FLAGS} /WX")
-endif()

 if (WIN32)
+    target_compile_options(onnx_proto PRIVATE
+        /MP
**+        /MX**
+    )
     target_compile_options(onnx PRIVATE
+        /MP
**+        /MX**
         /wd4800 # disable warning type' : forcing value to bool 'true' or 'false' (performance warning)
         /wd4503 # identifier' : decorated name length exceeded, name was truncated
     )
     target_compile_options(onnx_pybind PRIVATE
+        /MP
**+        /MX**

I get this warning
  Warning	D9002	ignoring unknown option '/MX'	onnx_proto	

and I'm assuming it's a typo from the change given '/WX' was removed and '/MX' was added.

Correcting this would also require onnx_proto to ignore C4146 as /WX will treat warnings as errors.

issue_title: Google Net would not work for GPU
issue_body:
Dear Sir,
I have integrated GoogleNet, but I found it works on CPU, but I choose GPU, it failed. I don't know why. Might you please help check that. I'm using Microsoft's sample code,Windows Machine Learning Explorer, to integrate this model. the link is like below:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/uwp/machine-learning/samples
https://blogs.technet.microsoft.com/machinelearning/2018/03/13/how-three-lines-of-code-and-windows-machine-learning-empower-net-developers-to-run-ai-locally-on-windows-10-devices/

thanks a lot,
-Sam

> `dilations` : **list of ints**
> dilation value along each axis of the filter. If not present, the dilation defaults to 1 along each axis.

It is not clear that these axes don't include input/output channels. Some libraries such as TensorFlow _do_ represent strides in a 4-long list, so I think making this explicit would be good. Also relevant for `pads` and `strides`.

And:

> `kernel_shape` : **list of ints**
> The shape of the convolution kernel. If not present, should be inferred from input W.

Similar issue: I have always thought of convolutional filters as 4D, but from looking at the [tests](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/onnx/backend/test/case/node/conv.py), I guess all of these attributes refer only to 2 dimensions.

issue_title: How do I tell you guys about a feature I've added after forking?
issue_body:
I tried sending a patch in once, probably a year ago and was told that git was the way to do it, not patches.  But I can't figure out who to email.  I'm MikeSandford and I've added a command that'll make it easier to create either a mysql database or a postgresql database.  If you only setup a project once you can do it by hand.  I worked at a company for a while where we turned out a new site every couple of weeks so it came in quite handy.  The command spits out the necessary SQL to create the database, which you can pipe to your database and get it all done in a single line.

Here's about what it looks like:
./manage.py sqlcreate | mysql -u <db_administrator> -p  
./manage.py sqlcreate | psql -U <db_administrator> -W

Obviously I don't know if this will get included, but I'd like to make it available if you guys think it passes the "worth it" test and it's not full of fail.
